audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a woman phoning the owner of a house she is going to rent about companies that she could rent furniture from.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hi, this is Shelley Mayer.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'm renting your house on Archwood Avenue.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'm due to move in next week.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Oh yes, hello, Ms Mayer.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What can I do for you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: When I viewed the house, I told you I'll most probably need to rent some furniture - at least until I know whether my temporary work contract is going to be made permanent.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Yes, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I remember.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And I said I could give you some information about furniture rental companies in the city.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Well, the biggest company is called Peak Rentals.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I've recommended them to other people, and I've always heard positive reports about them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Could you give me an idea of their costs?
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Sure, I actually have one of their brochures here.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It says the monthly price per room starts at $105 and goes up to $239.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That depends on which rooms you need furniture for, of course.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Sure. It's just to get a general idea of how much it's gonna cost.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: And you said you had some positive feedback about this company.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Yes. People have mentioned that the furniture from Peak Rentals is more modern than any of the other companies.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And also, once you place an order, the furniture will be delivered to you in just 1 or 2 days.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That would be really helpful.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Oh, and the brochure says that there's a special offer at the moment, if you rent living room furniture.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I believe that's a set of chairs and a TV table.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You'll also get a lamp at no extra cost.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ok, but you know that price range you gave is more than I was hoping to pay.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Then you could try Aaron and Oliver.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Sorry, what and Oliver?
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Aaron, double A R O N.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Ok, are they cheaper?
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'd say they're a mid price company, but if you chose them, you need to be aware that they charge an extra 12% every month in case of damage.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'd have to do the math carefully then.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But one helpful thing is that they also do cleaning for customers.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: For the furniture?
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: For the house.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I see.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I probably won't need that.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: There's another company called Larch Furniture.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's quite new, and it has the lowest prices in town.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's for both furniture and also electronic equipment.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, that would be good.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'm not bringing much with me, and I won't have much time to go shopping after I start my job.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: There are two things you need to know about Larch Furniture.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: First of all, you have to take out insurance on the furniture, and you need to organize that yourself.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That wouldn't be too hard.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Also, you can't take out a contract for less than 6 months.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But I figure that might not be a problem for you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You're renting the house for 12 months after all, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes. OK. Well, I...
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Sorry to interrupt.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I just thought of another furniture rental company.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's called Space Rentals, and it's located very near to the house.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I don't have any information about their charges.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So it's best to use their app to find out what it would cost you to use them.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: I'll do that.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: One good thing about that company is that if you don't like the furniture once it's delivered, you can request exchanges, as long as you do that within a week of receiving it.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That sounds really great.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: Ok, well thanks very much, that's so helpful.
audioscript.speaker: SHELLEY MAYER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Archaeological Excavation of an Ancient Village

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear an archaeologist who is part of a community project to excavate an ancient village, talking to members of the public who are visiting the site.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, I'm Hayden.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: I'm one of the archaeologists investigating the site here at Bidcaster.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: This is the third summer for this community project.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: And most of the people digging here are volunteers.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: I'm a full-time archaeologist for the town council, but I was asked to join the project by NHA,
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: a charity which sets up projects like this up and down the country.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: As you can see, we're next to Bidcaster Castle, which is great, because the owners let us use their facilities.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: So, how did we get to where we are today?
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Many archaeology projects happen when an ancient object is found, and in our case that object was a gold coin.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Coins are often found by people using metal detectors to look for things buried in the ground, or coins are uncovered when wild animals like rabbits have been digging tunnels.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Here, a walker found it on the ground after a rainstorm washed away some of the earth and sand.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: When the story of the gold coin hit the news, Peter Swift, an amateur historian, contacted me to say he believed there had been a village on this site.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Centuries before the castle was built.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Just by chance, the team found some old maps and documents in our library, which showed 500-year-old drawings of ruined buildings on the grassy area between the outer stone walls of the castle and the river.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: We knew then we were onto something.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Over the three summers the team has been here, we found the remains of several buildings, and more broken pots than you can count.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Normally you'd expect to find brooches and other jewelry.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: But we're still waiting to uncover any such items.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: The people who once lived here were skilled at making tools from animal bones, as you'll see when you visit the exhibition.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Besides the discovery of the village, we've also found evidence of human activity on the other side of the river.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: No other houses or huts so far.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: But we can see the borders of an ancient field system.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: At one point we found a long wall and thought it was an ancient palace, but it turned out to be a modern wall.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: This summer's work will end soon, but we'll be back next summer.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: In the meantime, we're putting on a series of guided tours for school groups this autumn.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Oh, and um maybe you saw the TV documentary about our project.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: That suggested the objects we found are going to the town's museum, but we don't know that for sure yet.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: When you enter the site, please make sure you keep to the paths at all times.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: There are a few other things, um the highlights of the site if you like that I want to mention.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Take a look at the map.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Our present location is marked at the bottom.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: This year, we've identified the foundations of an ancient bridge, and it's really exciting today because a team of divers are in the river searching for lost objects.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: To reach the bridge, take the main path ahead of you, go straight on, and keep going till the path bends to the left.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: You'll see a smaller track leading off to the right.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Follow that to take you to the river where the divers are.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: You might be interested to see the rubbish pit.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: This is very near the castle walls in the northwest corner of the site.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: It actually dates to the time of the castle and not the ancient village.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: We found oyster shells and fish bones, and we assumed they were thrown from the castle kitchen above.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: One area we excavated in the first summer uncovered the site of a meeting hall.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: We knew it was an important building, because it had two rows of post holes.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Deep enough to support a large roof.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: It's the largest structure in the central area of the site, next to the current excavation area.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Last year we discovered a fish pond in the ancient village.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Normally these were beside a river.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: The pond here is further away, but it's possible the river has moved slightly.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Anyway, to get there from here, you turn right at the first information board you come to, and follow the path into the trees.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: Before you come out of the trees, you'll see it on your right.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: If you reach the river, you've gone too far.
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: So does anyone have an...
audioscript.speaker: HAYDEN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: It actually dates to the time of the castle and not the ancient village.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: We found oyster shells and fish bones, and we assumed they were thrown from the castle kitchen above.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: One area we excavated in the first summer uncovered the site of a meeting hall.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: We knew it was an important building, because it had two rows of post holes.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: Deep enough to support a large roof.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: It's the largest structure in the central area of the site, next to the current excavation area.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: Last year we discovered a fish pond in the ancient village.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: Normally these were beside a river.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: The pond here is further away, but it's possible the river has moved slightly.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: Anyway, to get there from here, you turn right at the first information board you come to, and follow the path into the trees.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: Before you come out of the trees, you'll see it on your right.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: If you reach the river, you've gone too far.
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: So does anyone have an...
utterances.speaker: HAYDEN

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Who was responsible for starting the community project?
options: the castle owners
a national charity
the local council

items.title: How was the gold coin found?
options: Heavy rain had removed some of the soil.
The ground was dug up by wild rabbits.
A person with a metal detector searched the area.

items.title: What led the archaeologists to believe there was an ancient village on this site?
options: the lucky discovery of old records
the bases of several structures visible in the grass
the unusual stones found near the castle

items.title: What are the team still hoping to find?
options: everyday pottery
animal bones
pieces of jewellery

items.title: What was found on the other side of the river to the castle?
options: the remains of a large palace
the outline of fields
a number of small huts

items.title: What do the team plan to do after work ends this summer?
options: prepare a display for a museum
take part in a television programme
start to organise school visits

test_text.title: Theater Studies Students' Discussion on Theater Programs

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two theater studies students, Maya and Finn, discussing their project on theater programs.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Finn, I've done as much as I can for our project on theater programs.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: How's your research coming along?
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Ok, Maya.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: I didn't know theater programs are called playbills in the USA, till I started looking into the topic.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Even though I struggled to find many useful websites, I'm glad we picked this subject.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: No one else on the course is doing the same as us, although it is one of the research areas of the module convenor.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: That might actually put some people off.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Huh, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Anyway, I hadn't realized there are actually companies specializing in creating theater programs.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Yes, they're quite common nowadays.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Contrary to what many people think, theatres don't hire people to do the programs.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: In fact companies buy the rights to publish programs on the theatre's behalf.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: And then make their money selling advertising space within the program booklet.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: It must be easier for theaters to do it that way.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I remember reading something about programs in early British theater.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: It said that the cast was always very important.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Yeah, audiences were very familiar with leading actors, and big names would draw huge crowds.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: But I hadn't realized that if the program named a famous actor, that's who the public expected to perform, and if that didn't happen, people accused the theater of breaking their agreement with the audience.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: They would demand refunds, and if they didn't get them, there were riots.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Outrageous. That'd never happen now.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: No, people are too polite, even when they're disappointed if the star of the show misses a performance.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: We should definitely include that information about early audiences in our project.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I also think it's important to mention that lots of ordinary people at that time were illiterate, so theater programs were of limited value in advertising plays.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: When a company of actors arrived in a town, they'd parade around the streets in their costumes, beating drums and announcing their upcoming performances.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Interesting.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: I couldn't imagine that happening now either.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: There's also an interesting comparison to make between 18th and 19th century programs.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Wasn't it in the 19th century that theater programs started to resemble programs today?
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: And unlike programs from the 18th century, they always used color.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: And there was a greater variety of designs. But personally, I think 18th-century programs were superior, because they told the theater goers so many things, including about the actors.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: And about the writer, the plot, and sometimes the history of the play.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: What should we say about theater programs in the 20th century?
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: I reckon the most important thing is the dramatic change they underwent during World War 2.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: When the government imposed restrictions on the use of paper.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Yeah, but that was only in the UK, in the USA, programs or rather playbills continued to be published in the same format.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Well, here in the UK, programs became merely a single sheet of paper folded to create 4 pages for text.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: What I don't really get is that after the war, they didn't go back to being more than one sheet, or change in any way for over 25 years.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I know there were paper shortages after the war, but only for 5 or 10 years.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Hmm, strange.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've got some pictures of programs we could include on the slides for our presentation.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I found a couple too, Maya.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Let's go through and see what we think.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: um Oh, this is an old one, for a play called Ruy Blas.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Hmm, never heard of that, but the program looks very decorative.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Good enough to put in a frame on the wall.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: The images are just beautiful.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Finn, what did you find?
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I've got some pages from a program for Man of La Mancha.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: I thought this was a good program to show, not because of the pictures.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: But because it contains articles written by members of the theater company, so we can learn how the production was created, and the thoughts and feelings of the cast.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: I've got a copy of a program that's now in a museum.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: It's for The Tragedy of Jane Shore, and it's said to be the earliest surviving document to have been printed on Australia's first printing press.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Fantastic.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: Another program to talk about is for the Sailors Festival.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: It comes from the British Library's digitized collection of programs that was started a few years ago.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: It already comprises over 200,000 programs, which is amazing.
audioscript.speaker: MAYA

audioscript.text: Huh, wish I'd known about it while I was doing my research.
audioscript.speaker: FINN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Finn was pleased to discover that their topic
options: was not familiar to their module leader.
had not been chosen by other students.
did not prove to be difficult to research.

items.title: Maya says a mistaken belief about theatre programmes is that
options: theatres pay companies to produce them.
few theatre-goers buy them nowadays.
they contain far more adverts than previously.

items.title: Finn was surprised that, in early British theatre, programmes
options: were difficult for audiences to obtain.
were given out free of charge.
were seen as a kind of contract.

items.title: Maya feels their project should include an explanation of why companies of actors
options: promoted their own plays.
performed plays outdoors.
had to tour with their plays.

items.title: Finn and Maya both think that, compared to nineteenth-century programmes,those from the eighteenth century
options: were more original.
were more colourful.
were more informative.

items.title: Maya doesn't fully understand why, in the twentieth century,
options: very few theatre programmes were printed in the USA.
British theatre programmes failed to develop for so long.
theatre programmes in Britain copied fashions from the USA.

text: What comment is made about the programme for each of the following shows?
textReadable: What comment is made about the programme for each of the following shows?

list: It is historically signifi cant for a country.
It was effective at attracting audiences.

It contains insights into the show.
body.title: Show

test_text.title: Inclusive Design: Concepts, Examples and Consequences

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a design and technology lecturer talking about inclusive design.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: It's only relatively recently that designers have become aware of the need to be inclusive when designing products, but what does that mean exactly?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, it simply means designing products that span economic, social and cultural barriers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It means making sure products are accessible.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So that as many different types of people as possible can use them without any type of adaptation having to be made to the original design.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Inclusive design is often linked with universal design, although they are not quite the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Universal design aims to make products that work for everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that includes considering the needs of people who have cognitive difficulties, which can present quite a challenge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Today, examples of successful inclusive design can be seen all around us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In workplaces, it is common to see desks which can be adjusted to suit people of different heights, or for wheelchair users.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This still isn't always the case, however, and is one reason why office workers often suffer from back or neck problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll find another example in the public toilets of countless hotels, airports and offices.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Taps that you activate by sensor require no pressing or twisting movements.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are not only more hygienic, they're also easier for people with dexterity or mobility issues.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The tech industry has been criticized in the past for focusing too much on young consumers, but this is changing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many products are now designed with the elderly in mind.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, it's well known that vision declines with age, and that we also become worse at distinguishing between similar colors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In particular shades of blue, which is why software designers rarely create interfaces with this color.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Motor skills also decline with age.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And some people have difficulty doing everyday things, like picking up a cup or opening a door.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This can also affect their ability to use a mouse or keyboard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So voice access is now a routine way of making commands.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's worth looking at the problems non-inclusive designs cause when not enough consideration is given to a range of users, as it can have a serious impact on people's lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Access is one obvious example, because it has such a huge impact on disabled people's independence.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Not being able to access public transport because buses or trains are not wheelchair-friendly means many disabled people can't go out, unless someone goes with them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Safety is another issue, inexplicably, most cars are still crash tested using a dummy based on an average sized male.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This has safety implications for all women, particularly those who are pregnant, as the seat belts worn by the dummy are not adapted to accommodate them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Over the past 100 years, workplaces in the UK have on the whole become considerably safer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Employers are legally required to provide well maintained personal protective equipment or PPE.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Anything from goggles to full body suits, to workers who need it free of charge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But most PPE is designed to fit men.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A recent report found that employers often think that when it comes to female workers, all they need to do to comply with this legal requirement is to buy jackets for example designed for a small man.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The problem with this is that women can be tall and still have much smaller shoulders than the average man.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ill-fitting PPE such as hi-vis jackets vests and body armor can put women at risk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The report found that 95% of women said that their PPE often hampered their work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that this problem was worst in the emergency services, particularly the police.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another problem is related to comfort at work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A very common scenario in offices in summertime is to see women wrapped in blankets or wearing sweaters while the air conditioning is on high.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Meanwhile the men are in shorts and t-shirts.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is due to differences in metabolic rates for men and women.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There is a standard setting for air conditioning to be at a temperature of 21 degrees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Designed to suit men, and in most modern offices, it's not possible to turn the air conditioning up or down.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means that many offices which mainly employ women are wasting energy by having the air conditioning set too high.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you can see from the examples I've just mentioned, there are serious consequences for designs which don't consider the needs of all users.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is that is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In workplaces, it is common to see desks which can be adjusted to suit people of different heights, or for wheelchair users.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This still isn't always the case, however, and is one reason why office workers often suffer from back or neck problems.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll find another example in the public toilets of countless hotels, airports and offices.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Taps that you activate by sensor require no pressing or twisting movements.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are not only more hygienic, they're also easier for people with dexterity or mobility issues.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The tech industry has been criticized in the past for focusing too much on young consumers, but this is changing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many products are now designed with the elderly in mind.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, it's well known that vision declines with age, and that we also become worse at distinguishing between similar colors.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In particular shades of blue, which is why software designers rarely create interfaces with this color.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Motor skills also decline with age.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And some people have difficulty doing everyday things, like picking up a cup or opening a door.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This can also affect their ability to use a mouse or keyboard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So voice access is now a routine way of making commands.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's worth looking at the problems non-inclusive designs cause when not enough consideration is given to a range of users, as it can have a serious impact on people's lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Access is one obvious example, because it has such a huge impact on disabled people's independence.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Not being able to access public transport because buses or trains are not wheelchair-friendly means many disabled people can't go out, unless someone goes with them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Safety is another issue, inexplicably, most cars are still crash tested using a dummy based on an average sized male.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This has safety implications for all women, particularly those who are pregnant, as the seat belts worn by the dummy are not adapted to accommodate them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Over the past 100 years, workplaces in the UK have on the whole become considerably safer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Employers are legally required to provide well maintained personal protective equipment or PPE.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Anything from goggles to full body suits, to workers who need it free of charge.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But most PPE is designed to fit men.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A recent report found that employers often think that when it comes to female workers, all they need to do to comply with this legal requirement is to buy jackets for example designed for a small man.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The problem with this is that women can be tall and still have much smaller shoulders than the average man.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ill-fitting PPE such as hi-vis jackets vests and body armor can put women at risk.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The report found that 95% of women said that their PPE often hampered their work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that this problem was worst in the emergency services, particularly the police.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another problem is related to comfort at work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A very common scenario in offices in summertime is to see women wrapped in blankets or wearing sweaters while the air conditioning is on high.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Meanwhile the men are in shorts and t-shirts.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is due to differences in metabolic rates for men and women.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There is a standard setting for air conditioning to be at a temperature of 21 degrees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Designed to suit men, and in most modern offices, it's not possible to turn the air conditioning up or down.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This means that many offices which mainly employ women are wasting energy by having the air conditioning set too high.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As you can see from the examples I've just mentioned, there are serious consequences for designs which don't consider the needs of all users.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is that is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: Designing products that can be accessed by a diverse range of people without the need for any <input>.
Not the same as universal design: that is design for everyone, including catering for people with <input> problems.
items.title: Definition

items: <input> which are adjustable, avoiding back or neck problems
<input> in public toilets which are easier to use
people can make commands using a mouse, keyboard or their <input>.

items.title: Examples of inclusive design

items: Loss of independence for disabled people.
Seatbelts are especially problematic for <input> women.
PPE jackets are often unsuitable because of the size of women's <input>.
PPE for female <input> officers dealing with emergencies is the worst.
The <input> in offices is often too low for women.
